title: Real Estate Deals in St. Paul MN: A Complete Investor's Guide for 2024
excerpt: St. Paul, Minnesota offers real estate investors a steady market with affordable entry points, strong rental demand, and motivated sellers across diverse neighborhoods. This guide breaks down where to find the best deals, what to expect on returns, and how to get started even if you're investing from out of state.
author: AIWholesail Team
tags: real estate deals in St. Paul MN
St. Paul Minnesota real estate investing
fix and flip St. Paul MN
metaDescription: Find profitable real estate deals in St. Paul MN. Explore top neighborhoods, average prices, wholesale and rental strategies, and tips for out-of-state investors.
metaKeywords: real estate deals in St. Paul MN, St. Paul Minnesota real estate investing, wholesale real estate St. Paul, fix and flip St. Paul MN, rental properties St. Paul, off-market deals St. Paul, St. Paul real estate market 2024

sections.content: St. Paul, Minnesota doesn't always get the same spotlight as its twin city Minneapolis, but that's exactly what makes it interesting for real estate investors. Lower entry prices, stable rental demand, and a mix of older housing stock create real opportunity for wholesalers, flippers, and buy-and-hold investors alike. Whether you're a seasoned investor or just getting started with real estate investing for beginners, St. Paul is a market worth studying closely in 2024.
St. Paul MN Real Estate Market Overview

St. Paul is the state capital of Minnesota and home to roughly 310,000 residents. The city sits across the Mississippi River from Minneapolis and shares much of its economic foundation, including major employers in healthcare, education, and government. But St. Paul has its own housing market personality. Homes here tend to be older, with a large share of properties built before 1950. That aging housing stock creates a consistent pipeline of properties that need work, which is exactly what investors look for.

As of late 2024, the median home price in St. Paul sits around $250,000 to $275,000, noticeably lower than Minneapolis where medians push past $320,000. That price gap is a big reason why investors continue to target St. Paul for both flips and rentals. Days on market have been running between 25 and 45 days depending on the neighborhood and condition of the property, giving active investors enough time to move without missing deals entirely.
Best Neighborhoods for Real Estate Deals in St. Paul MN

Not all St. Paul neighborhoods are created equal from an investment standpoint. Some offer strong rental yields, others are better suited for fix-and-flip projects, and a few are transitioning fast enough to attract appreciation plays. Here are the neighborhoods most investors focus on when hunting for deals.
Frogtown and Thomas-Dale

Frogtown is one of the most active areas for wholesale and flip activity in St. Paul. Home prices regularly fall between $150,000 and $220,000, and you can find distressed properties well below that range. The neighborhood has a high concentration of renters, which supports landlord strategies as well. Gentrification has been slow but steady here, and investors who bought five years ago have seen solid appreciation on top of cash flow.
West Side and Dayton's Bluff

The West Side offers scenic river views and a tight-knit community feel. Properties here can be undervalued relative to their condition and location. Dayton's Bluff, sitting just east of downtown, is another pocket where you can find older homes needing cosmetic updates. After a solid rehab, ARVs in Dayton's Bluff often land between $230,000 and $290,000 depending on square footage and finish level. Before you commit to a rehab budget, run your numbers through our free ARV Calculator to pressure-test your after-repair value assumptions.
Hamline-Midway and Como

These central neighborhoods attract a different investor profile. Properties here are more stabilized, but you can still find deals from estate sales, tired landlords, and probate situations. Como especially benefits from proximity to the University of Minnesota St. Paul campus, which keeps rental demand consistent. Expect median prices in the $270,000 to $320,000 range, with strong rental rates that can support positive cash flow on the right purchase.
Types of Real Estate Deals Available in St. Paul
Wholesale Deals

Wholesaling is active in St. Paul because the inventory of distressed and dated properties gives wholesalers consistent deal flow. A good wholesale deal here might involve a property purchased under contract at $130,000 with an ARV of $220,000, leaving room for a $15,000 to $25,000 assignment fee after accounting for rehab costs and the end buyer's margin. If you're new to this strategy, check out this guide on wholesaling real estate for beginners before you start making offers.
Fix and Flip

Flippers are active throughout St. Paul's older neighborhoods. The typical renovation scope involves updating kitchens, bathrooms, flooring, and mechanicals in homes built between 1900 and 1960. Budget rehab costs of $40,000 to $80,000 depending on condition, and target neighborhoods where ARVs support a healthy margin. With a purchase at $160,000, a $55,000 rehab, and holding costs around $12,000, you'd need an ARV of at least $260,000 to hit a 15 percent profit margin. Use our free Deal Calculator to quickly run these scenarios before you make an offer.
Buy-and-Hold Rentals

St. Paul has a strong rental market. The city's homeownership rate hovers around 50 percent, meaning half the population rents. Average rents for a 3-bedroom single-family home run between $1,600 and $2,100 per month depending on neighborhood and condition. At those rent levels, properties purchased in the $200,000 range can produce a 1 percent monthly rent ratio, which is the traditional benchmark for cash-flowing rentals. Multi-family properties, particularly duplexes and triplexes, are also available and often provide better cash flow than single-family homes on a per-unit basis.

Pro Tip: St. Paul has specific rental licensing requirements. Before closing on any rental property, confirm the property is licensed or can be licensed with the city. Properties with deferred maintenance or code violations may face delays in getting licensed, which directly affects your cash flow timeline.
How to Find Off-Market Deals in St. Paul

The best real estate deals in St. Paul MN are rarely listed on the MLS at a discount. Off-market sourcing is where most serious investors focus their energy. Direct mail campaigns targeting absentee owners, probate leads, and tax-delinquent properties consistently produce motivated sellers in St. Paul's older neighborhoods. Driving for dollars in Frogtown or Dayton's Bluff and noting properties with deferred maintenance is another proven tactic. For a deeper breakdown of these strategies, read our guide on how to find off-market real estate deals.

items: Target absentee owners in zip codes 55104, 55106, and 55107 for the highest density of motivated sellers
Pull probate filings from Ramsey County District Court, which handles estates for St. Paul properties
Check the St. Paul city website for properties with delinquent utility accounts, which often signals a distressed owner
Network with local estate sale companies who often know about properties before they hit the market
Connect with St. Paul-based real estate attorneys who handle divorce and estate cases

sections.content: St. Paul Real Estate Market Trends to Watch in 2024

Interest rates have reshaped buyer behavior across Minnesota, and St. Paul is no exception. Higher rates have pushed some would-be buyers back into renting, which strengthens the landlord's position. At the same time, fewer buyers mean less competition for investors making cash offers on distressed properties. The light rail Green Line running through Midway continues to influence values along that corridor, with properties near transit stops holding up better than outlying areas during slower periods.

Housing inventory in St. Paul has been historically low, but listings have ticked up slightly through 2024 compared to the tight pandemic-era market. That modest increase in inventory gives buyers a little more negotiating room than they had in 2021 and 2022, when bidding wars were common even on distressed properties. For investors, that shift in leverage is meaningful. Visit our dedicated St. Paul MN market page for regularly updated deal data and market metrics.

"The best time to buy real estate in a market like St. Paul is when other investors aren't paying attention. The fundamentals — population, employment, and rental demand — are stable. The deals are there for people willing to do the work to find them."
Tips for Out-of-State Investors Targeting St. Paul

St. Paul is a realistic target for remote investors, but it requires building the right local team. Minnesota's cold winters are hard on properties, so deferred maintenance issues like roofs, furnaces, and plumbing need to be at the top of your inspection checklist. A property that looks fine in July can have significant winter-related issues that don't show up without a thorough inspection.

items: Hire a local property manager before you close your first deal, not after — interviews with three or more companies to compare fee structures and tenant screening standards
Work with a St. Paul-based real estate attorney familiar with Minnesota landlord-tenant law, which favors tenants in several key areas
Build relationships with local contractors who know how to winterize properties and handle cold-weather rehabs on realistic timelines
Understand that Minnesota has a 6-month redemption period on foreclosures, which affects the timeline on certain deal types
Visit the market at least once before scaling up — walk target neighborhoods and meet your team in person to vet them properly
Learn how to analyze deals correctly before making remote offers; read our guide on how to analyze real estate deals like a pro to sharpen your evaluation process

sections.content: Pro Tip: When analyzing St. Paul rentals remotely, add a 10 percent buffer to your estimated repair costs for cold-weather considerations. Older homes in this market frequently have aging boiler systems, outdated electrical panels, and foundation issues that are easy to underestimate from afar.

St. Paul rewards patient, systematic investors who do their homework. The city offers lower price points than most comparable Midwest metros, a durable rental base, and consistent deal flow from an aging housing stock. For investors willing to build local relationships and run disciplined numbers, the market continues to produce real opportunities in 2024 and beyond.
